text: Mason Currey's research into the daily routines of 161 creative figures, published as 'Daily Rituals,' revealed a counterintuitive pattern: the most productive workers were rarely those who worked the longest hours. Darwin walked the same gravel path for hours each day thinking; Kant rose at 5am and lectured until noon; Beethoven counted exactly 60 coffee beans each morning. These rituals share a structural feature: they impose predictability on everything except the work itself. By automating peripheral decisions — when to rise, what to eat, when to begin — creative practitioners preserved their limited decision-making capacity for the work that actually required it.
question: According to the passage, what structural feature do the rituals of Darwin, Kant, and Beethoven share?

options.text: They all began before sunrise to take advantage of peak cognitive hours
They imposed predictability on everything except the work itself
They involved physical movement as a precondition for intellectual effort
They restricted social interaction to specific hours of the afternoon
